author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "But to pass rapidly through these minor points: can he be said to lay down the law who is not a magistrate of the court? or is what he lays down to be considered law, when in the character of a private person he disturbs public rights?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Is it not rather the case that he not only involves himself in guilt, but is held to be a forger, and that which he composes a forgery?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: What if your private person, whom you deem a forger, were to set forth to any one the law of the emperor?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Would not the man, when he had compared it with the law of those who have the genuine law, and found it to be identically the same, lay aside all care about the source from which he had obtained it, and consider only what he had obtained?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For what the forger gives is false when he gives it of his own falseness; but when something true is given by any person, even though he be a forger, yet, although the giver be not truthful, the gift is notwithstanding true.
